name: Mongol Empire Yuan branch: ᠶᠡᠬᠡ ᠮᠣᠩᠭᠣᠯ ᠤᠯᠤᠰ → 元朝 → 北元 (1206–1635)
description: East-Asian dynastic branch of the Mongol imperial system: the Yekhe Mongol Ulus (ᠶᠡᠬᠡ ᠮᠣᠩᠭᠣᠯ ᠤᠯᠤᠰ, 'Great Mongol Nation') founded at the 1206 Onon River kurultai under Genghis Khan (Činggis Qa'an), formally partitioned among Genghis's four sons into the Four Khanates; the East-Asian ulus becomes the Yuan dynasty (大元, 1271–1368) under Khubilai Khan after he proclaims the dynastic name 'Da Yuan' at Khanbaliq (modern Beijing) on 18 December 1271 with reference to the I Ching's phrase 'dade qian yuan' (the great originating principle); the Yuan is expelled from China by the Ming rebellion of Zhu Yuanzhang (Hongwu emperor) in 1368 and retreats to the Mongol steppe as the Northern Yuan (北元, 1368–1635), which maintains the Chinggisid imperial title and a nominal succession until its last khan Ligdan Khan dies of smallpox in flight from the Manchu (Later Jin) forces in 1634 and his son Ejei Khan surrenders the Yuan imperial seal to Hong Taiji in 1635 — transmitting Chinggisid legitimacy into the Qing dynasty (which would appear on the Chinese dynastic chain, batch_04). The chain tracks ONE of the four Mongol-ulus branches; the parallel Golden Horde (Ulus of Jochi, 1227–1502), Chagatai Khanate (1227–1347), and Ilkhanate (1256–1335) are documented as entity records and could form parallel chains in a future release.
ethical_notes: Five points of care. (a) The 1206 founding of the Mongol Empire initiated 60 years of the most extensive and demographically catastrophic conquests in pre-industrial world history. Estimates of total Mongol-conquest mortality range from 20–60 million direct deaths (Matthew White's Great Big Book of Horrible Things assigns ~40 million; other sources go as high as 60 million for the combined Mongol campaigns 1206–1279). Specific atrocities include: the sack of Otrar (1219–1220, ~100,000 dead following the execution of Mongol envoys by the Khwarazmshah's governor); the sack of Balkh, Bamiyan, Nishapur, and Merv (1220–1221 — Merv's population was variously estimated at 700,000 to 1.3 million before the Mongol destruction; modern scholarship treats the higher figures as exaggerated but accepts that Merv went from Central Asia's largest pre-modern city to near-total depopulation); the destruction of the Jin Dynasty (northern China, 1211–1234) with depopulation estimates for north China exceeding 50%; the 1240 sack of Kiev (Киῤв), effectively ending Kievan Rus' as an organised polity; the 1241 Battle of Mohi and destruction of Hungary (~20–25% of the Hungarian population killed); the 1258 sack of Baghdad (already documented on the Islamic chain). (b) Despite this founding violence, the Mongol imperial period produced the 'Pax Mongolica' (Pax Tatarica) — the first continuous overland trade-and-communication corridor from China to the Mediterranean, the jarghu legal system, the yam postal relay system, extensive religious toleration (Nestorian Christians, Buddhists, Muslims, Confucians, and Taoists all held senior court positions under various khans), and transmission of technologies (gunpowder, printing, medicine) from China to the Islamic world and Europe. The chain includes both the catastrophic founding violence AND these structural contributions; each of the two aspects is historically real and cannot be erased for the comfort of the other. (c) The Yuan dynastic proclamation (1271) was the first non-Han Chinese dynasty to rule all of China, instituting a formal four-caste system: Mongols, Semu (Central Asian Muslim and Christian administrators), Han (northern Chinese), and Nanren (southern Chinese, the last-conquered Southern Song population). The caste system subordinated the majority Han and Nanren populations in ways that fed resentment and contributed to the Red Turban rebellion (1351 onward) that eventually produced the Ming. (d) The 1368 Ming expulsion of the Yuan was presented by Ming propaganda as a civilisational restoration (fu Hua / 'restore the Chinese'), but this ideological framing served Han ethnic chauvinism that was itself weaponised against Mongols living in China (regular Ming-era anti-Mongol pogroms) and later against other non-Han populations. The Northern Yuan's persistence to 1635 is minimised in most Ming-centric historiography. (e) The 1635 submission of Ejei Khan to Hong Taiji transferred Chinggisid legitimacy to the Qing, which used it to claim sovereignty over Mongolia and to justify the later Qing conquest of the Zunghar Khanate (1755–1758) — an explicit Mongol-on-Mongol genocide carried out by the Qing-Manchu state against the last independent Mongol polity, killing ~80% of the Zunghar population (estimated ~600,000 of ~750,000 Zunghars killed, with the survivors absorbed into Qing banner populations or Kazakh/Kyrgyz groups). The Zunghar genocide appears on the Chinese dynastic chain's Qing-link ethical notes (batch_04).

sources.citation: The Secret History of the Mongols (Монггол-ун Нитоуча Тобчиян, c. 1240) — primary for Genghis's life and the early empire.
Ata-Malik Juvayni (d. 1283). تاریخ جهانگشا (Tarikh-i Jahangushay / History of the World-Conqueror) — primary for the Central Asian campaigns.
Rashid al-Din Hamadani (d. 1318). جامع التواریخ (Jami' al-tawarikh / Compendium of Chronicles) — Ilkhanid universal history, canonical for Mongol dynastic genealogies.
Yuan Shi (元史, 1370, compiled under the Ming by Song Lian et al.) — the official dynastic history of the Yuan.
Marco Polo (d. 1324). Il Milione / Divisament dou monde — primary Western source for Khubilai-era China.
Altan Tobchi (὾ǙȀȳ-὾ֽםֺ֢ד, c. 1655) — primary Mongol chronicle for the Northern Yuan and Chinggisid genealogy.
Morgan, D. (1986, 2nd ed. 2007). The Mongols. Blackwell — canonical modern synthesis.
Rossabi, M. (1988). Khubilai Khan: His Life and Times. California UP.
Biran, M. (2005). The Empire of the Qara Khitai in Eurasian History. Cambridge UP.
Allsen, T. (2001). Culture and Conquest in Mongol Eurasia. Cambridge UP — central for the Pax Mongolica argument.
Jackson, P. (2005). The Mongols and the West, 1221–1410. Pearson.
May, T. (2012). The Mongol Conquests in World History. Reaktion.
Perdue, P. (2005). China Marches West: The Qing Conquest of Central Eurasia. Harvard UP — for the 1635 transfer and Zunghar context.

links.description: The Great Mongol Empire founded at the spring 1206 kurultai on the Onon River, at which Temüjin — having unified the Mongolian steppe tribes through the wars documented in the Secret History (defeating the Tatars 1202, Keraites 1203, and Naimans 1204–1205) — was proclaimed Činggis (Genghis) Qa'an, 'Universal Ruler.' Under Genghis (r. 1206–1227), the empire destroyed the Western Xia (1205–1210, 1226–1227 final campaign), the Jin Dynasty's northern territories (1211–1234, final conquest under Ogedei), the Qara Khitai Khanate (1218), and the Khwarazmian Empire (1219–1221, following the Khwarazmshah's execution of Mongol envoys at Otrar). Under Ogedei (r. 1229–1241), Batu Khan's Western Campaign (1236–1242) destroys the Volga Bulgars, the Rus' principalities (sack of Riazan 1237, sack of Vladimir 1238, sack of Kiev 1240), and devastates Hungary (Mohi 1241) and Poland (Legnica 1241); the campaign halts only at Ogedei's death (11 December 1241) when Batu returns for the kurultai. Under Mongke (r. 1251–1259), Hulagu's campaign destroys the Nizari Ismaili Assassins of Alamut (1256) and sacks Baghdad (1258). The empire is formally partitioned among Genghis's sons and grandsons into the Four Uluses: Jochi (Golden Horde), Chagatai, Hulagu (Ilkhanate), and Toluy (whose line produces the Yuan under Khubilai and the Great Khan line).
links.ethical_notes: The 60-year Mongol conquest period (1206–1260s) killed an estimated 20–60 million persons worldwide — in absolute numbers the second-largest single sequence of demographic catastrophe in pre-industrial history (after the Columbian Exchange 1492 onward). Specific documented atrocities include: Otrar (1219–1220, ~100,000 killed after the city's population refused surrender), Merv (1221, contemporary chroniclers' figures of 700,000–1,300,000 civilian dead are exaggerated but modern scholarship accepts ~200,000+ and near-total destruction of what had been one of Islamic civilisation's largest cities), Nishapur (1221, reportedly ordered razed to the ground after a Mongol prince's death in battle, with piles of skulls made from the dead), Bamiyan (1221, the 'city of sorrow' per contemporary accounts, reportedly no survivors), Ryazan (December 1237, first Rus' city taken by Batu, ~total destruction per the Tale of the Destruction of Ryazan), Kiev (December 1240, ~2,000 of 50,000 inhabitants survived per archaeological estimates), Mohi (April 1241, Hungarian army destroyed, ~20–25% Hungarian population mortality from the combined invasion), and Baghdad (1258, documented on batch_05). The conquests also initiated the Black Death transmission from the steppe to the Mediterranean via Mongol siege warfare (the 1346 Kaffa siege, during which plague victims were catapulted into the besieged Genoese city) — the subsequent pandemic killed an estimated 75–200 million in Eurasia and North Africa 1346–1353. The Pax Mongolica did enable the Silk Road transmission that Allsen (2001) documents; this does not mitigate the mortality of the founding but is a separate structural fact. The chain's ethical notes above synthesise these tensions; this link focuses on the specific founding atrocities of the unified Mongol imperial period.

links.description: The Yuan Dynasty, proclaimed by Khubilai Khan (忽必烈, r. 1260–1294) at Khanbaliq (modern Beijing) on 18 December 1271, adopting the dynastic name 大元 ('Great Yuan') from the I Ching. Khubilai's succession had been contested — he was elected Great Khan at the 1260 Kaiping kurultai, but his brother Ariq Boke was simultaneously elected at Karakorum, producing the Toluid Civil War (1260–1264) which Khubilai won. This fractured the unified Mongol imperial structure: the Chagatai Khanate and Golden Horde became effectively independent. The Yuan completed the conquest of southern China by destroying the Southern Song at the Battle of Yamen (19 March 1279), in which the nine-year-old Emperor Bing drowned with his minister Lu Xiufu rather than surrender. Yuan rule (1271–1368) instituted the four-caste system (Mongol / Semu / Han / Nanren), ran China via Mongol and Semu administrators with Confucian Han officials in subordinate roles, maintained the yam postal system, extensively employed Tibetan Buddhism (the 'Phags-pa lama became Imperial Preceptor), and hosted the Khubilai-era Marco Polo visit (c. 1275–1292). The dynasty declined rapidly after ~1340 through succession crises (nine emperors in 38 years), Yellow River flooding and famine, the Black Death's arrival in China (probably 1331), and the Red Turban rebellion (from 1351) which produced the peasant rebel Zhu Yuanzhang — the future Hongwu emperor of the Ming.
links.ethical_notes: The 1279 Yamen battle killed an estimated 100,000 Song loyalists and civilians (many jumping into the sea with the imperial court rather than surrender). The subsequent Yuan demographic impact on southern China is debated — census figures show northern China's registered population declining from ~50 million in 1195 (Jin) to ~10 million in the 1290s (Yuan), though part of this reflects under-registration rather than actual mortality; southern China's population impact was less severe. The four-caste system subordinated ~90% of the Chinese population (Han + Nanren) to structural disadvantage in law, office-holding, and taxation — Han/Nanren were subject to more severe criminal penalties, could not hold senior military positions, and were excluded from some administrative posts. Tibetan 'Phags-pa Buddhism was institutionally favoured, producing tensions with Confucian and Taoist establishments that the Red Turban rebellion would weaponise. The 1351–1368 collapse killed millions through famine and warfare; the Hongwu emperor's founding of the Ming in 1368 explicitly framed itself as 'expelling the Hu, restoring China' (驅逐胡虐，恢复中华) — an ethnic-restoration narrative that has been repeatedly reactivated in modern Chinese nationalism.

links.description: The Northern Yuan (北元), the Yuan successor state on the Mongolian steppe, conventionally dated from the flight of Toghon Temur (the last Yuan emperor) from Khanbaliq on 10 September 1368, as Ming forces under Xu Da approached the capital. Toghon Temur retreated to Karakorum and then further north to Yingchang and Ulaanbaatar; his line maintained the Yuan imperial title and bureaucratic continuity in vestigial form until its effective collapse. The Northern Yuan saw the Oirat-Borjigin civil wars (15th century), the rise and fall of the Oirat Esen Taishi (r. 1438–1454, briefly unified Mongolia and captured the Ming Zhengtong emperor at Tumu in 1449), Dayan Khan's reconsolidation (r. 1479–1517, reestablished Chinggisid hegemony), and Altan Khan's renewed campaigns against Ming (1550 Altan's sack of the Beijing suburbs) and adoption of Tibetan Buddhism (1577 Gelugpa alliance, bestowal of the 'Dalai Lama' title on Sonam Gyatso). The line terminated with Ligdan Khan (r. 1604–1634), the last Mongol great khan, who attempted to reunify the steppe against the rising Manchu power of Nurhaci and Hong Taiji but was defeated; Ligdan died of smallpox in 1634 fleeing toward Tibet, and in 1635 his son Ejei Khan surrendered the Yuan imperial seal to Hong Taiji — transmitting Chinggisid imperial legitimacy to the Manchu Qing dynasty, which used it to claim suzerainty over all Mongols.
links.ethical_notes: The Northern Yuan period saw sustained low-intensity warfare along the Ming-Mongol frontier, with the Ming constructing the Great Wall's Ming-era sections explicitly as anti-Mongol defence — these defensive walls cost millions of corvée labourers' lives across the 15th–16th centuries. The Oirat civil wars (15th c.) produced substantial Mongol-on-Mongol violence. The 1449 Tumu Crisis — Esen's defeat of the Ming expeditionary army and capture of the Zhengtong emperor — killed an estimated 50,000 Ming troops and a half-century of Ming political trauma (the subsequent 'Hongxi Restoration' / Yingzong's reinstatement after Esen released him in 1450 was a major constitutional crisis). The Northern Yuan's adoption of Tibetan Buddhism (1577 Altan–Sonam Gyatso meeting) and the bestowal of the Dalai Lama title fundamentally restructured Tibetan religious politics, making the Gelugpa the dominant Tibetan Buddhist school under successive Mongol patronage. The 1635 submission of Ejei Khan to the Manchu was presented in Qing ideology as a voluntary Mongol homage; in Mongol historiography it is often treated as the endpoint of steppe political autonomy, with the subsequent Qing conquest of the Zunghar Khanate (1755–1758) as the formal extinguishing of independent Mongol polity. The Zunghar genocide (~600,000 deaths, 80% of the population) is the companion episode to this chain's termination, documented on the Chinese dynastic chain (batch_04, Qing link).
